title: Upholstering a headboard with a plain fabric panel
subtitle: Wrap a plywood headboard blank in foam and Dacron then staple on cover fabric
excerpt: How to upholster a flat plywood headboard blank with upholstery foam, a Dacron wadding layer, and a cover fabric, using a staple gun for all fixings. Covers cutting the foam, wrapping with Dacron, fabric tensioning, and corner folding.
sourceNotes: City and Guilds Level 2 Furniture Upholstery. BFTA (British Furniture Traders Association) guidance on headboard construction. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

glossaryTerms.definition: The technique of stretching upholstery fabric tight across a panel before fixing it, pulling from the centre of each edge outward and from face to back alternately — not from corner to corner — so that tension is distributed evenly. Stapling corners before the centres is the most common cause of puckering; centres first, working outward, gives a drum-flat result.

content.text: to achieve a smooth surface. The common mistake is to staple the corners first and then pull the sides in; this pushes excess fabric to the mid-sections and creates diamond-shaped pleats. Centre-first stapling distributes the tension evenly. The plywood blank should be cut to the finished headboard shape before starting; any profiling of the top edge is much easier on bare ply.

18 mm plywood or MDF headboard blank, cut to finished shape. Medium-firm upholstery foam, 50 to 75 mm thick, cut to the same shape as the blank. Dacron polyester wadding to wrap over the foam. Cover fabric, a closely woven furnishing fabric with no more than 10% stretch. Fabric adhesive spray to hold the foam to the blank. Stainless or galvanised 10 mm T50 staples.
Step 1, cut and attach the foam

Cut the foam to the same outline as the plywood blank using the upholstery shears or a sharp serrated knife. Spray fabric adhesive onto the front face of the blank and press the foam firmly in place. Allow the adhesive to tack before handling. The foam should sit flush with the edges of the blank on all sides.

Cut a piece of Dacron wadding 150 mm larger all around than the blank. Lay the wadding flat, place the blank foam-side down in the centre, and fold the Dacron over the edges onto the back of the blank. Staple the Dacron to the back of the blank at the top centre, then the bottom centre, then the left centre, then the right centre, pulling it just firm enough to remove wrinkles, not so tight that it compresses the foam. Work outward from each centre staple, stapling at 50 mm intervals. Fold the corners neatly as for wrapping a parcel and staple flat.
Step 3, cut and position the cover fabric

Cut the cover fabric 200 mm larger all around than the blank to allow for pulling around the edges. Check the fabric grain is running straight across the headboard face before stapling anything. Lay the fabric face-down on a flat surface, place the Dacron-wrapped blank Dacron-side down on top of it, and centre it.

Fold the fabric over the top edge at the centre and place one staple through the fabric and Dacron into the back of the plywood. Fold and staple the bottom centre, pulling firmly to remove any slack on the face. Then staple the left and right centres. Continue working outward from each centre staple, pulling the fabric firmly and evenly before each staple, until all four sides are stapled at 30 to 40 mm intervals. At each corner, fold the fabric into a neat pleat, two diagonal tucks folded flat, one from each adjacent side, and staple. Trim any excess fabric that extends beyond the staple line.

attrs.symptom: Fabric shows diagonal puckers or smile lines across the face
attrs.cause: Tension was applied in one direction before the opposite direction was established, pulling the grain off-square.
attrs.fix: Remove the staples from the affected section using a staple remover. Re-staple starting from the centre of the puckered run, pulling the fabric firmly in the direction that removes the pucker, and work outward.

attrs.symptom: Foam edge shows through the fabric as a visible step at the headboard perimeter
attrs.cause: The Dacron layer is too thin or was pulled too tight over the foam edge, flattening it.
attrs.fix: Use a thicker Dacron layer — 100 g/m² minimum for a padded headboard edge. At the perimeter, leave the Dacron slightly looser to allow it to wrap around the foam edge without compressing it flat.
